patches.replace:
and that 0.0 µGal eclipse measurements confirm the model (WIN-013/014). The dome's own reconciliation — the raw-text source notes describe WIN-013/014 as "Confirms electromagnetic, not gravitational" — is self-defeating: if the eclipse effect is electromagnetic rather than gravitational, then the −6.5 µGal denominator in κ is not a gravity anomaly either, and κ as a "magnetic-gravity coupling" is conceptually incoherent.

The derivation is also circular
patches.rationale: Adds dome's own reconciliation text from raw-text/02-wins.txt to strengthen the incoherence argument

patches.find:
A genuine coupling constant requires simultaneous measurement of both coupled quantities at the same location during the same event.

The monitor.py code validation
patches.replace:
A genuine coupling constant requires simultaneous measurement of both coupled quantities at the same location during the same event. Even the −6.5 µGal value itself is suspect on the dome's own terms: Wang et al. (2000) report the gravity "valleys" at first and last contact, not at eclipse maximum, and explicitly state "no significant anomaly during the very solar eclipse was found" — opposite to what gravitational shielding would predict (peak at maximum coverage). See WIN-011.

The monitor.py code validation
patches.rationale: Imports Wang et al. (2000) timing finding from WIN-011 to undermine the Mohe datum itself

patches.find: GRACE measures inter-satellite K-band range changes, not local gravity in μGal — that unit belongs to terrestrial superconducting gravimeters
patches.replace: The unit on GRACE Level-1A products is not μGal — L1A is pre-inversion instrument data (accelerations in m/s² from ACC1A, K-band range rates from KBR1A, star-camera quaternions from SCA1A; see JPL PO.DAAC conventions). μGal is a post-processing unit applied to retrieved time-variable gravity at surface points. A plot labeled both "raw L1A" and "μGal" on the y-axis is self-inconsistent — it is labeling raw instrument data with a derived-product unit
patches.rationale: Tightens GRACE sentence to be more precise and defensible against technical counter-arguments

patches.find: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
patches.replace: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The 1.574c figure was published by Tesla in 1905 (US Patent 787412) and restated in his Colorado Springs Notes (Nikola Tesla Museum, Beograd 1978); the value has been in the scientific record for over a century. The dome catalog adopts this published number verbatim and labels it CONFIRMED, without pre-registering the expected value or identifying a novel measurement that could not have been known at write-time. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time" — a century-old patent value does not qualify.
patches.rationale: WIN-062 has monitoring=none; current boilerplate incorrectly references a monitor script. Replace with Tesla 1905 patent framing appropriate for this WIN.

patches.find: the 10–13 µGal signal is within 1997-era instrumental noise; Van Camp et al. (2001) found no eclipse gravity signal with four superconducting gravimeters
patches.replace: the 6–7 µGal Wang signal is within 1997-era LaCoste-Romberg instrumental noise; Van Camp et al. (2001) found no eclipse gravity signal with four superconducting gravimeters (~1,000× more precise)
patches.rationale: 10-13 uGal is the dome's predicted value; 6-7 uGal is what Wang et al. (2000) actually measured. Cite observed not predicted, add precision comparison.
